original_hypothesis.title: FCL ecAff_add uses ecAff_IsZero(y==0) identity shortcut that could be exploitable if reused for non-P256 curves or non-prime order contexts
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: FCL.ecAff_IsZero considers any point with y==0 as the point-at-infinity. For P-256 with prime order (cofactor 1), such points should not exist in the prime-order subgroup, but if the library is ever reused or adapted (or if an invalid point slips through), this identity shortcut can cause incorrect additions/multiplications, potentially enabling signature verification false positives/negatives.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: FreshCryptoLib is explicitly warned against non-prime order curves. Still, code is in scope; if any call path can pass a point with y==0 that still satisfies ecAff_isOnCurve (or if ecAff_isOnCurve is bypassed), this could corrupt verification results.
deployment_assumptions: FCL is reused beyond strict P-256 prime-order assumptions, or an upstream caller fails to enforce ecAff_isOnCurve before calling ecAff_add/ecZZ_mulmuladd_S_asm
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Library comments warn about non-prime order curves; current WebAuthn path uses FCL.ecdsa_verify which checks ecAff_isOnCurve, reducing exploitability for P-256.

proof_of_signal.lines: ecAff_IsZero(uint256, uint256 y) returns (y==0) as identity; ecAff_add uses ecAff_IsZero to shortcut addition
proof_of_signal.pattern: identity test based only on y coordinate

preconditions: Caller reaches ecAff_add with a point where y==0 but point is not intended to represent infinity in that context
Either curve/group permits such points in practice or input validation is insufficient
Result of point ops influences signature verification or authorization

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: incorrect ECC arithmetic under certain inputs/curves
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: identity should typically be (x==0 && y==0) or explicit infinity encoding

analysis_questions: For secp256r1, can ecAff_isOnCurve accept any point with y==0 (should be impossible in prime subgroup)?
Are there any internal call sites that use ecAff_add without first checking ecAff_isOnCurve?
Could malformed inputs reach lower-level functions directly in future upgrades or other integrations?
validation_requirements: Mathematically verify existence/nonexistence of y==0 points on P-256 curve modulo p
Fuzz Qx,Qy to see if any on-curve points with y==0 pass ecAff_isOnCurve
Check all internal uses of ecAff_add

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: If a y==0 on-curve point could be passed, ecAff_add may treat it as infinity and produce wrong multiplication results, potentially making ecdsa_verify accept invalid signatures or reject valid ones in a different curve context.
prerequisites: Nonstandard curve use or validation gap in upstream caller

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Could be severe if it leads to signature forgery in some deployment context
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any system relying on FCL for signature verification under affected curve assumptions

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No explicit tests covering identity edge cases in point arithmetic

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
